behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of relevance score
behaviors.justification: Availability status is stored in a product database. A Unit test with mocked product data could pass even if the real database query is broken (wrong column name, missing index, failed join). An Integration test using a real/test database catches both query failures and filtering logic errors.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with products, some marked availability=false. Call the recommendation algorithm. Verify all returned products have availability=true; no out-of-stock products appear in results even if they would rank high.

behaviors.description: User preference filter excludes product categories explicitly blocked in user settings
behaviors.justification: Blocked categories are stored in a user settings database. A Unit test mocking preferences could pass even if the settings query is broken or schema mismatched. Integration testing against a real/test database verifies both the query and the filtering logic work together.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with a user who has blocked categories {X, Y, Z}. Call the recommendation endpoint. Verify no products from blocked categories appear in results, regardless of relevance.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: Browsing history is persisted in a database. A Unit test with mocked history could pass even if the history query is broken (wrong date range, missing filter, JOIN failure). Integration testing ensures both the history retrieval and the ranking logic are correct.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with a user who has browsed categories {electronics, books}. Load a catalog with products across multiple categories. Call the algorithm. Verify products from electronics/books rank higher than unvisited categories.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if data unchanged
behaviors.justification: Pagination stability depends on deterministic database query ordering. A Unit test with a fixed in-memory list would not catch the failure 'database query returns results in different order on different calls' (typical if ORDER BY is missing). Integration testing with a real database catches database-level ordering failures.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendation API twice with identical parameters, requesting page 2 both times. Verify results are byte-identical: same product IDs in same order, same scores, same pagination metadata.

behaviors.description: Algorithm surfaces at least one product from category user hasn't browsed (serendipity), avoiding filter bubble
behaviors.justification: Serendipity is an algorithmic logic requirement. With a seeded random number generator, Unit tests can deterministically verify the algorithm includes unvisited categories. The failure 'algorithm never includes serendipity logic' is caught at Unit level. Category data itself can be mocked in the test.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history in only categories {A, B}. Seed the RNG deterministically. Call the ranking algorithm. Verify at least one product from category {C, D, E, ...} appears in results.

behaviors.description: New user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity, not empty results
behaviors.justification: The fallback logic (use popularity when history is empty) is pure algorithm logic with no I/O dependency. A Unit test can verify the branching logic: given empty history, return products ordered by a popularity metric.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user object with zero purchase history and zero browsing history. Call the ranking algorithm directly. Verify response is non-empty and products are sorted by popularity metric (or expected fallback metric).

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each user interaction
behaviors.justification: Click tracking involves writing data to persistent storage (database or event log). A Unit test mocking storage would pass even if real writes fail (connection error, schema mismatch, permissions). Integration testing against a real/test database verifies the write actually succeeds and data is retrievable.
behaviors.test_description: Set up test database with empty click_events table. Simulate user clicking product_id=42 at position 3. Query click_events table. Verify a record exists with product_id=42, position=3, and a valid timestamp (within reasonable bounds).

behaviors.description: Response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance depends on realistic data volume and execution environment. A Unit test with 100 products cannot reveal bottlenecks that emerge at 100k scale (index misses, algorithm complexity, memory pressure). System-level testing with real or representative data and measurement infrastructure is required.
behaviors.test_description: Provision test environment with a catalog of 100,000 products and 10,000 users with varied history. Call recommendation endpoint repeatedly for different users. Measure wall-clock response time. Verify 95th percentile latency is under 200ms; flag any individual request exceeding 200ms.

behaviors.description: When recommended product is purchased, algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: The behavior involves state change in persistent storage: purchase recording must update the user's profile, then the algorithm must query that profile and adjust ranking. A Unit test with mocked purchase history could pass even if purchase-recording fails or the history-query is broken. Integration verifies the full write-and-read cycle.
behaviors.test_description: Set up test database with user U. Generate recommendations (product A should appear). Record a purchase of product A for user U. Generate recommendations again. Verify product A is either absent from results or ranked significantly lower than before.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for same user state — requesting twice with no intervening activity returns identical ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism is an algorithmic property: given fixed input state, output must be identical. Unit tests can verify this by calling the algorithm twice with the same state and checking for bit-identical output. If the algorithm uses randomness, seeding ensures determinism. Failures like 'random seed not set' or 'timestamp included in output' are caught at Unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Call the ranking algorithm twice with identical user state and a seeded RNG. Verify both calls return identical results: same product IDs, same order, same scores. Run this test multiple times with different seeds to ensure determinism holds across random states.
